author: John Chrysostom
book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

ccel.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

title: Homily XVI on Acts vii. 6, 7.

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And God spake on this wise, That his seed should sojourn in a strange land; and that they should bring them into bondage, and entreat them evil four hundred years.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the nation to whom they shall be in bondage will I judge, said God: and after that shall they come forth, and serve Me in this place.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See, what a number of years the Promise has been given, and the manner of the Promise, and nowhere sacrifice, nowhere circumcision!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He here shows, how God Himself suffered them to be afflicted, not that He had anything to lay to their charge.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they shall bring them into bondage,” etc. But nevertheless, they did not these things with impunity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the nation to whom they shall be in bondage I will judge, said God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, to show that they are not to go by this, in estimating who are pious (by reason of their saying, “He trusted in God, let Him deliver Him,”) (Matt. xxvii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 43).—He, the Same that promised, He that gave the land, first permits the evils.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So also now, though He has promised a Kingdom, yet He suffers us to be exercised in temptations.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If here the freedom was not to be till after four hundred years, what wonder, with regard to the Kingdom?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yet he performed it, and lapse of time availed not to falsify His word.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moreover, it was no ordinary bondage they underwent.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the matter does not terminate solely in the punishment of those (their oppressors); but they themselves also, He saith, shall enjoy a mighty salvation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here he reminds them too of the benefit which they enjoyed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he gave him the covenant of circumcision: and so he begat Isaac.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here he lets himself down to lower matters.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And circumcised him on the eighth day: and Isaac (begat) Jacob, and Jacob the twelve patriarchs.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 8).—Here he seems to hint now at the type.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the patriarchs moved with envy, sold Joseph into Egypt.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here again, the type of Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Though they had no fault to find with him, and though he came on purpose to bring them their food, they thus ill-treated him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Still here again the promise, though it is a long while first, receives its fulfillment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And God was with him”—this also is for them—“and delivered him out of all his afflictions.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows that unknowingly they helped to fulfil the prophecy, and that they were themselves the cause, and that the evils recoiled on their own selves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And gave him favor and wisdom in the sight of Pharaoh king of Egypt, Gave him favor,” in the eyes of a barbarian, to him, the slave, the captive: his brethren sold him, this (barbarian) honored him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now there came a dearth over all the land of Egypt and Canaan, and great affliction: and our fathers found no sustenance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when Jacob heard that there was corn in Egypt, he sent out our fathers first.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And at the second time Joseph was made known to his brethren.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They came down to buy, and had to depend upon him for everything.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then did he? [“He made himself known to his brethren:”] not to this point only did he carry his friendliness; he also made them known to Pharaoh, and brought them down into the land.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Joseph’s kindred was made known unto Pharaoh.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then sent Joseph, and called his father Jacob to him, and all his kindred, threescore and fifteen souls.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So Jacob went down into Egypt, and died, he, and our fathers, and were carried over into Sychem, and laid in the sepulchre that Abraham bought for a sum of money of the sons of Emmor the father of Sychem.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when the time of the promise drew nigh, which God had sworn to Abraham the people grew and multiplied in Egypt, till another king arose, which knew not Joseph.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then again, fresh disappointment (ἀνελπιστία): first, famine, but they came through that: secondly, the falling into the hands of their enemy: thirdly, the being destroyed by the king.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then (to show) God’s fulness of ways and means (εὐμήχανον), “In which time,” it says, “Moses was born, and was exceeding fair.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If the former circumstance was wonderful, that Joseph was sold by his brethren, here again is another circumstance more wonderful still, that the king “nourished” the very person who was to overthrow his dominion, being himself the person that was to perish.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you observe all along a figurative enacting, so to say, of the resurrection of the dead?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But it is not the same thing for God himself to do a thing, and for a thing to come to pass in connection with man’s purpose (προαίρεσις).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For these things indeed were in connection with man’s purpose [but the Resurrection by itself, independently.]—“And he was mighty,” it says, “in word and in deed” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22): he that was to have died.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then again he shows how ungrateful they were to their benefactor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, just as in the former instance, they were saved by the injured Joseph, so here again they were saved by another injured person, I mean, Moses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he was full forty years old,” etc. For what though they killed him not actually?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In intention they did kill, as did the others in the former case.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There, they sold out of their own into a strange land: here, they drive from one strange land into another strange land: in the former case, one in the act of bringing them food; in this, one in the act of giving them good counsel; one to whom, under God, the man was indebted for his life!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how it shows (the truth of) that saying of Gamaliel’s, “If it be of God, ye cannot overthrow it.” (ch. v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See the plotted-against eventually becoming the authors of salvation to those plotting against them: the people, plotting against itself, and itself plotted against by others; and for all this, saved!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A famine, and it did not consume them: nor was this all: but they were saved by means of the very person, whom they had expected to be destroyed (by their means).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A royal edict, and it did not consume them: nay then most did their number increase, when he was dead “who knew” them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Their own Saviour they wished to kill, but for all that, they had not power to do it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you observe, that by the means whereby the devil tried to bring to naught the promise of God, by those very means it was advanced?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And God spake on this wise,” etc. (Recapitulation, v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is suitable to be said here also: that God is rich in ways and means to bring us up from hence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this above all showed the riches of God’s resources, that in its very reverses (ἀποστροφῇ) the nation increased, while enslaved, while evil-entreated, and sought to be exterminated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this is the greatness of the Promise.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For had it increased in its own land, it had not been so wonderful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And besides, it was not for a short time, either, that they were in the strange land: but for four hundred years.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hence we learn a (great lesson) of philosophic endurance (φιλοσοφίαν):—they did not treat them as masters use slaves, but as enemies and tyrants—and he foretold that they should be set in great liberty: for this is the meaning of that expression, “They shall serve (Me): and they shall come up hither again” (ἐνταὕθα ἐπανελεύσονται); and with impunity.—And observe, how, while he seems to concede something to circumcision, he in fact allows it nothing (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 8); since the Promise was before it, and it followed after.—“And the patriarchs,” he says, “moved with envy.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Where it does no harm, he humors (χαρίζεται) them: for they prided themselves much on these also.—And he shows, that the saints were not exempt from tribulation, but that in their very tribulations they obtained help.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And that these persons did themselves help to bring about the results, who wished to cut short these same (afflictions): just as these made Joseph the more glorious: just as the king did Moses, by ordering the children to be killed: since had he not ordered, this would not have been: just as also that (Hebrew) drives Moses into exile, that there he may have the Vision, having become worthy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus also him who was sold for a slave, makes He to reign as king there, where he was thought to be a slave.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus also does Christ in His death give proof of His power: thus also does He there reign as king where they sold Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And gave him favor and wisdom,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This was not only by way of honor, but that he should have confidence in his own power.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he made him governor over Egypt and all his house.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now there came a dearth,” etc. On account of famine—such preparations is he making—“with threescore and fifteen souls,” he says, “Jacob went down into Egypt, and died, he and our fathers, and were carried over into Sychem, and laid in the sepulchre that Abraham bought for a sum of money from the sons of Emmor the father of Sychem.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It shows, that they were not masters even to the extent of a burying-place.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But when the time of the promise drew nigh, which God had sworn to Abraham, the people grew and multiplied in Egypt, till another king arose, which knew not Joseph” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, that it is not during the four hundred years that He multiplies them, but (only) when the end was about to draw nigh.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet already four hundred years were passed, nay more, in Egypt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this is the wonder of it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The same dealt subtly with our kindred, and evil-entreated our fathers, that they should cast out their young children, to the end they might not live.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Dealt subtly:” he hints at their not liking to exterminate them openly: “that they should cast out their young children,” it says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “In which time Moses was born and was exceeding fair.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the wonder, that he who is to be their champion, is born, neither after nor before, these things, but in the very midst of the storm (θυμῷ).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And was nourished up in his father’s house three months.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when man’s help was despaired of, and they cast him forth, then did God’s benefit shine forth conspicuous.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he was cast out, Pharaoh’s daughter took him up, and nourished him for her own son.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not a word of Temple, not a word of Sacrifice, while all these Providences are taking place.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he was nourished in a barbarian house.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Moses was learned in all the wisdom of the Egyptians, and was mighty in words and in deeds.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Was trained,” both in discipline and in letters.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he was full forty years old.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Forty years he was there, and was not found out from his being circumcised.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, how, being in safety, they overlook their own interests, both he and Joseph, in order that they may save others: “And when he was full forty years old, it came into his heart to visit his brethren the children of Israel.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And seeing one of them suffer wrong, he defended him, and avenged him that was oppressed, and smote the Egyptian: for he supposed his brethren would have understood how that God by his hand would deliver them: but they understood not.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 23–25.)—See how up to this point he is not yet offensive to them; how they listened to him while he said all this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And “his face,” we read, “was as the face of an angel” (ch. vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 15).—“For he supposed,” etc. And yet it was by deeds that his championship was shown; what intelligence was there need of here? but still for all this “they understood not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the next day he showed himself unto them as they strove, and would have set them at one again, saying, Sirs, ye are brethren; why do ye wrong one to another?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark with what mildness he addresses them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He who had shown his wrath in the case of the other, shows his gentleness in his own case.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But he that did his neighbor wrong thrust him away, saying, Who made thee a ruler and a judge over us?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wilt thou kill me, as thou didst the Egyptian yesterday?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark; the very words which they said to Christ: “Who made Thee ruler and judge over us?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So habitual a thing was it for Jews to wrong (their benefactors) when in the act of receiving benefits!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again, mark the atrocious baseness: (μιαρίαν al. μοχθηρίαν, Sav. marg.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “As thou didst the Egyptian yesterday!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then fled Moses at this saying, and was a stranger in the land of Midian, where he begat two sons.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But neither did flight extinguish the plan of Providence, as neither did death (i.e. the death of Christ).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when forty years were expired, there appeared to him in the wilderness of mount Sinai an angel of the Lord in a flame of fire in a bush.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark that it is not hindered by lapse of time?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when he was an exile, when a stranger, when he had now passed much time in a foreign land, so as to have two sons, when he no longer expected to return, then does the Angel appear to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Son of God he calls an Angel, as also he calls Him man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Appears) in the desert, not in a temple.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how many miracles are taking place, and no word of Temple, no word of Sacrifice.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And here also not simply in the desert, but in the bush.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “When Moses saw it, he wondered at the sight: and as he drew near to behold it, the voice of the Lord came unto him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo! he was deemed worthy of the Voice also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I am the God of thy fathers, the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo! how He shows that He is none other than “the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob”—He, “the Angel of the Great Counsel.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Wonderful, Counsellor,” E.V.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here he shows what great loving-kindness God herein exhibits.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then Moses trembled, and durst not behold.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then said the Lord to him, Put off thy shoes from thy feet; for the place where thou standest is holy ground.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not a word of Temple, and the place is holy through the appearance and operation of Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Far more wonderful this than the place which is in the Holy of Holies: for there God is nowhere said to have appeared in this manner, nor Moses to have thus trembled.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And then the greatness of His tender care.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I have seen, I have seen the affliction of My people which is in Egypt, and I have heard their groaning, and am come down to deliver them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And now come, I will send thee into Egypt.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See, how he shows, that both by kindnesses, and by chastisements, and by miracles, God was drawing them to Him: but they were still the same.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That God is everywhere present, they learned.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hearing these things, let us in our afflictions flee to Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And their groaning,” saith He, “I have heard:” not simply, “because of their calamities.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if any should ask, Why then did He suffer them to be evil entreated there?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, in the first place, to every just man his sufferings are the causes of his rewards.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And in the next place, as to why He afflicted them: it was to show His power, that He can (do all), and not only so, but that He may also train them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe in fact; when they were in the desert, then they “waxed fat, they grew thick, they spread out in breadth, they kicked” (Deut. xxxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 15): and ever and always ease was an evil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore also from the beginning He said to Adam: “In the sweat of thy face thou shall eat thy bread.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Also (it was) in order that having come out of much suffering into rest, they might give thanks to God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For affliction is a great good.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For hear the Prophet saying, “It is good for me, that Thou hast humbled me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if to great and wonderful men affliction be a great (good), much more to us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, if you will, let us examine into the nature of affliction as it is in itself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let there be some person rejoicing exceedingly, and gay, and giving a loose to jollity: what more unseemly, what more senseless than this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let there be one sorrowing and dejected: what more truly philosophic than this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, “It is better,” we read, “to go into the house of mourning, than into the house of laughter.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Eccles. vii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, likely enough, you do not like the saying, and want to evade it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us however see, what sort of man Adam was in Paradise, and what he was afterwards: what sort of man Cain was before, and what he was afterwards.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The soul does not stand fast in its proper place, but, like as by a running tide, (ῥεύματος, Edd. πνεύματος, “wind”) is raised and buoyed up by pleasure, having no steadfastness; facile in making professions, prompt at promising; the thoughts all in restless commotion: laughter ill-timed, causeless hilarity, idle clatter of unmeaning talk.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why speak of others?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us take in hand some one of the saints, and let us see what he was while in pleasure, what again, when in distress.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Shall we look at David himself?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When he was in pleasure and rejoicing, from his many trophies, from his victory, from his crowns, from his luxurious living, from his confidence, see what sort of things he said and did: “But I said in my prosperity,” says he, “I shall never be moved.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when he has come to be in affliction, hear what he says: “And if He say to me, I have no mind for thee; lo! here am I, let Him do that which is pleasing in His sight.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What can be more truly philosophic than these words?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Whatsoever may be pleasing to God,” saith he, “so let it be.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again he said to Saul: “If the Lord stirreth thee up against me, may thy sacrifice be acceptable.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (1 Sam. xxvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And then too, being in affliction, he spared even his enemies: but afterwards, not friends even, nor those who had done him no injury.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, Jacob when he was in affliction, said: “If the Lord will give me bread to eat, and raiment to put on.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Gen. xxviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As also the son of Noah did nothing of the kind erewhile; but when he was no longer afraid for his safety, you hear how wanton he became. (ib. ix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hezekiah too, when he was in affliction, see what things he did in order to his deliverance; he put on sackcloth, and such like; but when he was in pleasure, he fell through the haughtiness of his heart.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (2 Kings ch. xix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, saith the Scripture, “When thou hast eaten, and drunk, and art filled, take heed to thyself.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For perilous, as on a precipice’s brink, is the post of affluence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Take heed,” saith he, “to thyself.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When the Israelites were afflicted, they became all the more increased in number: but when He left them to themselves, then they all went to ruin.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why speak of examples from the ancients?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In our own times, let us see, if you please, is it not the case, that when the most are in good case, they become puffed up, hostile to everybody, passionate, while the power is with them: but if it be taken away, they are gentle, lowly (and as) human beings, are brought to a consciousness of their own natural condition.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore the Scripture saith, “Pride hath holden them unto the end: their iniquity shall go forth as from fatness.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Ps. lxxiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now these things I have spoken, that we should not make enjoyment every way our object.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then does Paul say, “Rejoice alway?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not say simply, “Rejoice,” but he adds, “in the Lord.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the greatest joy, such as the Apostles rejoiced withal; the joy of which prisons, and scourges, and persecutions, and evil report, and all painful things, are the source, and the root, and the occasion; whence also it comes to a happy issue.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But that of the world, on the contrary, begins with sweets and ends in bitters.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Neither do I forbid to rejoice in the Lord, nay, I earnestly exhort to this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Apostles were scourged, and they rejoiced: were bound, and they gave thanks: were stoned, and they preached.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the joy I also would have: from nothing bodily has it its origin, but from spiritual things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not possible for him who joys after the fashion of the world, to rejoice also after a godly sort: for every one who joys after the world’s fashion, has his joy in riches, in luxury, in honor, in power, in arrogance: but he who rejoices after the mind of God, has his joy in dishonor for God’s sake, in poverty, in want, in fasting, in humbleness of mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou, how opposite are the grounds (of joy)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To go without joy here, is to be without grief also: and to be without grief here, is to go without pleasure too.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And in truth these are the things which produce real joy, since the others have the name only of joy, but they altogether consist of pain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What misery the arrogant man endures!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How is he cut short (διακόπτεται) in the midst of his arrogance, bespeaking for himself numberless insults, much hatred, great enmity, exceeding spite, and many an evil eye!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whether it be that he is insulted by greater men, he grieves: or that he cannot make his stand against everybody, he is mortified.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whereas the humble man lives in much enjoyment: expecting honor from none, if he receive honor, he is pleased, but if not, he is not grieved.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He takes it contentedly that he is honored; but above all, none dishonors him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now not to seek honor, and yet to be honored—great must be the enjoyment of this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But in the other, it is just the reverse: he seeks honor, and is not honored.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the pleasure that the honor gives is not the same to him who seeks it, as it is to him who seeks it not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The one, however much he receives, thinks he has received nothing: the other, though you give him ever so little, takes it as though he had received all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then again, he who lives in affluence and luxury has numberless affairs of business, and let his revenues flow in to him ever so easily, and, as it were, from full fountains, yet he fears the evils arising from luxurious living, and the uncertainty of the future: but the other is always in a state of security and enjoyment, having accustomed himself to scantiness of diet.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he does not so bemoan himself at not partaking of a sumptuous board, as he luxuriates in not fearing the uncertainty of the future.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the evils arising from luxurious living, how many and great they are, none can be ignorant: it is necessary, however, to mention them now.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Twofold the war, in the body, and in the soul: twofold the storm: twofold the diseases; not only in this respect, but because they are both incurable, and bring with them great calamities.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not so, frugality: but here is twofold health, twofold the benefits.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Sleep of health,” we read, “is in moderate eating.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Ecclus. xxxi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For everywhere, that which keeps measure is pleasant, that which is beyond measure, ceases to please.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say now: on a little spark put a great pile of fagots, and you will no longer see the fire shining, but much disagreeable smoke.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On a very strong and large man lay a burden which exceeds his strength, and you will see him with his burden lying prostrate on the ground.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Embark too large a freight in your vessel, and you have ensured a grievous shipwreck.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just so it is here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For just as in overladen ships, great is the tumult of the sailors, the pilot, the man at the prow, and the passengers, while they cast into the sea the things above deck, and things below; so here too, with their vomitings upwards, and their purgings downwards, they mar their constitutions, and destroy themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what is the most shameful of all, the mouth is made to do the office of the nether parts, and that becomes the more shameful member.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if to the mouth the disgrace be such, think what must it be in the soul!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For indeed there it is all mist, all storm, all darkness, great the uproar of the thoughts, at being so thronged and crushed, the soul itself crying out at the abuse done to it: all (the parts and faculties) complaining of one another, beseeching, entreating, that the filth may be discharged somewhere.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And after it is flung out, still the turmoil is not at an end; but then comes fever and diseases.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And how comes it,” say you, “that one may see these luxurious livers, in goodly plight, riding on horseback?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What idle talk is this,” say you, “to tell us of diseases?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is I that am diseased, I that am racked, I that am disgusting, while I have nothing to eat.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ah me! for one may well lament at such words.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the sufferers with the gout, the men that are carried on litters, the men that are swathed with bandages, from what class of people, I ask you, shall we see these?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And indeed, were it not that they would deem it an insult, and think my words opprobrious, I would before now have addressed them even by name.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But there are some of them, who are in good health as well.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because they give themselves not merely to luxurious living, but also to labors.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Else show me a man, who does nothing whatever but fatten himself, free from pain as he lies there, without an anxious thought.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For though a host of physicians without number came together, they would not be able to rescue him from his diseases.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not in the nature of things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For I will hold you a medical discourse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of the matters sent down into the belly, not all becomes nourishment; since even in the food itself, not all is nutritive, but part of it in the process of digestion passes into stool, part is turned into nourishment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If then in the process of digestion the operation is perfect, this is the result, and each finds its proper place; the wholesome and useful part betakes itself to its appropriate place, while that which is superfluous and useless, withdraws itself, and passes off.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if it be in too great quantity, then even the nutritive part of it becomes hurtful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, to speak by way of example, in order that my meaning may be clearer to you: in wheat part is fine flour, part meal, part bran: now if the mill be able to grind (what is put in), it separates all these: but if you put in too much, all becomes mixed up together.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wine again, if it go through its proper process of formation, and under due influence of the seasons, then, whereas at first all is mixed together, anon part settles into lees, part rises into scum, part remains for enjoyment to those that use it, and this is the good part, and will not readily undergo any change.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But what they call “nourishment,” is neither wine, nor lees, while all are mixed up together.—The same may be seen in the river, when its waters make a whirling flood.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As at such time we see the fishes floating at top, dead, their eyes first blinded by the muddy slime: so is it with us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when gormandizing, like a flood of rain, has drenched the inward parts, it puts all in a whirl, and makes that the faculties (λογισμοὶ), healthy till then and living in a pure element, drift lifeless on the surface.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since then by all these examples we have shown how great the mischief is, let us cease to count these men happy for that, for which we ought to think them wretched, and to bemoan ourselves for that, for which we ought to count ourselves happy, and let us welcome sufficiency with a contented mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or do you not hear even what physicians tell you, that “want is the mother of health?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But what I say is, that want is mother, not of bodily health, but also of that of the soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These things Paul also, that physician indeed, cries aloud; when he says, “Having food and raiment, let us therewith be content.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us therefore do as he bids us, that so, being in sound health, we may perform the work that we ought to do, in Christ Jesus our Lord, with Whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost together be glory, dominion, honor, now and ever, world without end.
